questions.question_text: Under Louisiana law, what is a servitude?
questions.correct_answer: A charge on property benefiting an estate or person
distractors: A tax lien recorded by the parish clerk
A lease agreement between neighboring owners
A building restriction imposed by local zoning
questions.explanation: A servitude in Louisiana civil law is a charge on property for the benefit of another. A predial servitude is a charge on a servient estate for the benefit of a dominant estate (Civil Code article 646); a personal servitude, such as usufruct, habitation, or a right of use, is a charge on a thing for the benefit of a person (article 534). Predial servitudes correspond to what common-law states call easements.

questions.question_text: A predial servitude benefits which of the following?
distractors: A specific individual personally
The parish government
The servient estate owner
questions.explanation: A predial servitude is attached to land and benefits the dominant estate. It runs with the land and transfers with ownership. This is distinguished from a personal servitude, which benefits a specific person rather than a parcel of land.

questions.question_text: A property owner grants a neighbor a right of passage across her land. This is an example of what?
distractors: A personal servitude of usufruct
A building restriction
A right of habitation
questions.explanation: A right of passage granted to a neighboring property is a predial servitude because it benefits the neighboring estate, not just the individual neighbor. The servitude will transfer with both properties when they are sold.

questions.question_text: Which of the following can establish a servitude in Louisiana?
distractors: Only a recorded written agreement
Only a court order after a lawsuit
Only a parish zoning ordinance
questions.explanation: Under Civil Code article 654, predial servitudes may be natural (arising from the natural situation of estates, such as drainage), legal (imposed by law), or voluntary/conventional. Conventional servitudes are established by juridical act (agreement), by acquisitive prescription, or by destination of the owner. So law, agreement, and prescription are all valid paths to a servitude.

questions.question_text: A neighbor has used a path across an adjacent lot openly and continuously for the prescriptive period without permission. The lot owner now wants to block the path. What is the likely outcome?
questions.correct_answer: The neighbor may have acquired a servitude
distractors: The lot owner can block it at any time
The neighbor must obtain a parish permit
The path reverts to the lot owner automatically
questions.explanation: Under Louisiana law, a servitude can be established by prescription through open, continuous, and uninterrupted use for the required statutory period. If the neighbor meets these requirements, a servitude by prescription may have been acquired and the lot owner cannot block the path.

questions.question_text: What does a usufruct grant the usufructuary?
questions.correct_answer: The right to use property and enjoy its fruits
distractors: Full ownership of the property outright
The right to sell the property to others
Only the right to occupy it as a dwelling
questions.explanation: A usufruct grants the usufructuary the right to use and enjoy the property of another, including collecting its fruits (such as rent or crops), while the naked owner retains ownership. The usufructuary cannot alienate the property itself.

questions.question_text: What is the distinction between a perfect and imperfect usufruct?
questions.correct_answer: Perfect applies to nonconsumables, imperfect to consumables
distractors: Perfect is recorded, imperfect is unrecorded
Perfect lasts forever, imperfect is temporary
Perfect is granted by court, imperfect by will
questions.explanation: A perfect usufruct covers nonconsumable things that can be used without destroying them, such as a house or land; an imperfect (or quasi) usufruct covers consumable things such as money or harvested crops. The current Civil Code (articles 535-538) uses the terms usufruct of nonconsumables and usufruct of consumables: the usufructuary of consumables becomes their owner and must, at termination, return things of the same quantity and quality or pay their value.

questions.question_text: A husband dies intestate, survived by his wife and their children. By law, the wife receives a usufruct over his share of the community property, including the family home. When does this legal usufruct terminate?
questions.correct_answer: When she dies or remarries, whichever occurs first
distractors: When the children reach age 18
Ten years after the husband's death
When the mortgage on the home is paid off
questions.explanation: Under Civil Code article 890, when a spouse dies survived by descendants, the surviving spouse has a legal usufruct over the decedent's share of the community property to the extent it was not disposed of by testament. This legal usufruct terminates when the surviving spouse dies or remarries, whichever occurs first. By contrast, a usufruct left to the spouse by testament lasts for life unless the will provides a shorter term (article 1499), and any usufruct in favor of a natural person ends no later than the usufructuary's death (article 607).

questions.question_text: A usufructuary collects $2,000 monthly rent from a tenant occupying the property. Who is entitled to this rent?
questions.correct_answer: The usufructuary keeps the rent as fruits
distractors: The naked owner receives all rent
Rent is split equally between both parties
The parish collects rent during usufruct
questions.explanation: The usufructuary is entitled to the fruits of the property, which include rental income. Collecting rent is a fundamental right of the usufructuary. The naked owner retains ownership but does not receive the fruits during the usufruct.

questions.question_text: A widow holds a usufruct over her late husband's immovable. She wants to sell the property outright to a developer. The children, who are naked owners, object. Can she complete the sale?
questions.correct_answer: No, the usufructuary cannot sell the property
distractors: Yes, the usufructuary has full disposal rights
Yes, if the sale price exceeds appraised value
Only if a parish judge approves the sale
questions.explanation: A usufructuary has the right to use and enjoy the property but cannot alienate it. The naked owners hold title and must consent to any sale. The widow can sell or lease her usufruct interest, but she cannot convey the full ownership without the children's agreement.

questions.question_text: Under Louisiana law, the right of habitation allows a person to do what?
distractors: Sell the property to a third party
Lease the property for rental income
Modify the structure without consent
questions.explanation: The right of habitation is a personal servitude that allows the holder to dwell in another person's house. It is limited to the personal housing needs of the holder and is nontransferable.

questions.question_text: Can a holder of a right of habitation lease the dwelling to a third party?
questions.correct_answer: No, the right is personal and nontransferable
distractors: Yes, with the owner's written consent
Yes, for up to one year at a time
Only if the holder is temporarily absent
questions.explanation: The right of habitation is strictly personal and cannot be transferred, leased, or assigned to another person. The holder may only use the dwelling for personal residential needs, not for generating rental income.

questions.question_text: How does a right of use differ from a right of habitation in Louisiana?
questions.correct_answer: A right of use can benefit a legal entity and is transferable
distractors: A right of use is limited to dwelling in another's house
A right of habitation may be freely leased to third parties
There is no legal difference between the two rights
questions.explanation: A right of use confers a specified use of an estate less than full enjoyment (Civil Code article 639). Unlike habitation, it may be established in favor of a natural person or a legal entity (article 641), it is transferable unless prohibited by law or contract (article 643), and it is heritable unless the contrary is provided (article 644). Habitation, by contrast, is the nontransferable real right of a natural person to dwell in the house of another and may not be alienated, let, or encumbered (articles 630 and 637).

questions.question_text: Building restrictions in a Louisiana subdivision must meet which requirement to be enforceable?
questions.correct_answer: They must be written and recorded
distractors: Approved by the parish council only
Agreed to verbally by all lot owners
Filed with the state legislature
questions.explanation: Building restrictions in Louisiana must be established in writing and recorded in the parish conveyance records to be enforceable against subsequent purchasers. Unrecorded or verbal restrictions are generally not enforceable.

questions.question_text: A subdivision's building restrictions were recorded 30 years ago but have not been re-recorded or enforced. A new lot owner wants to build a structure that violates the original restrictions. Can the other owners enforce them?
questions.correct_answer: Possibly not, if the restrictions prescribed or were abandoned
distractors: Yes, recorded restrictions never expire
Yes, any owner can enforce at any time
No, restrictions expire after 10 years
questions.explanation: Louisiana building restrictions do not lapse merely from age, and there is no re-recording requirement. But under Civil Code article 781, no enforcement action may be brought more than two years after a noticeable violation begins — the immovable involved is then freed of the violated restriction — and under article 782, restrictions terminate by abandonment of the whole plan or general abandonment of a particular restriction. If past violations went unchallenged, the owners may no longer be able to enforce the restrictions.
